# ansys-tools-path

{{ pyansys }} {{ python }} {{ pypi }} {{ GH-CI }} {{ codecov }} {{ MIT }} {{ black }}

Library to locate Ansys products in a local machine.

% contribute_start

## How to install

At least two installation modes are provided: user and developer.

### For users

% howtoinstallusers_start

In order to install `ansys-tools-path`, make sure you
have the latest version of [pip]. To do so, run:

```bash
python -m pip install -U pip
```

Then, you can simply execute:

```bash
python -m pip install ansys-tools-path
```

% howtoinstallusers_end

### For developers

Installing `ansys-tools-path` in developer mode allows
you to modify the source and enhance it.

Before contributing to the project, please refer to the [PyAnsys Developer's guide].
You need to follow these steps:

1. Start by cloning this repository:

   ```bash
   git clone https://github.com/ansys/ansys-tools-path
   ```

2. Create a fresh-clean Python environment and activate it:

   ```bash
   # Create a virtual environment
   python -m venv .venv

   # Activate it in a POSIX system
   source .venv/bin/activate

   # Activate it in Windows CMD environment
   .venv\Scripts\activate.bat

   # Activate it in Windows Powershell
   .venv\Scripts\Activate.ps1
   ```

3. Make sure you have the latest required build system and doc, testing, and CI tools:

   ```bash
   python -m pip install .[tests]
   python -m pip install .[doc]
   python -m pip install .[build]
   ```

4. Install the project in editable mode:

   ```bash
   python -m pip install --editable ansys-tools-path
   ```

## How to testing

If required, you can always call the style commands ([black], [isort],
[flake8]...) or unit testing ones ([pytest]) from the command line. However,
this does not guarantee that your project is being tested in an isolated
environment, which is the reason why tools like [tox] exist.

### A note on pre-commit

The style checks take advantage of [pre-commit]. Developers are not forced but
encouraged to install this tool via:

```bash
python -m pip install pre-commit && pre-commit install
```

## Documentation

For building documentation, you can either run the usual rules provided in the
[Sphinx] Makefile, such us:

```bash
make -C doc/ html && your_browser_name doc/html/index.html
```

## Distributing

If you would like to create either source or wheel files, start by installing
the building requirements and then executing the build module:

```bash
python -m pip install .[build]
python -m build
python -m twine check dist/*
```

% LINKS AND REFERENCES

[black]: https://github.com/psf/black
[flake8]: https://flake8.pycqa.org/en/latest/
[isort]: https://github.com/PyCQA/isort
[pip]: https://pypi.org/project/pip/
[pre-commit]: https://pre-commit.com/
[pyansys developer's guide]: https://dev.docs.pyansys.com/
[pytest]: https://docs.pytest.org/en/stable/
[sphinx]: https://www.sphinx-doc.org/en/master/
[tox]: https://tox.wiki/

CLI tool to store the path of a solver.

USAGE:

The following example demonstrates the main use of this tool:

    $ save-ansys-path --name dyna /path/to/dyna

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._get_default_linux_base_path
documentation: Get the default base path of the Ansys unified install on linux.

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._get_default_windows_base_path
documentation: Get the default base path of the Ansys unified install on windows.

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._expand_base_path
documentation: Expand the base path to all possible ansys Unified installations contained within.

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._get_available_base_unified
documentation:
Get a dictionary of available Ansys Unified Installation versions with
their base paths.

# Returns

> Paths for Unified Installation versions installed.

# Examples

On Windows:

```pycon
>>> _get_available_base_unified()
>>> {231: 'C:\\Program Files\\ANSYS Inc\\v231'}
```

On Linux:

```pycon
>>> _get_available_base_unified()
>>> {231: '/usr/ansys_inc/v231'}
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_available_ansys_installations
documentation:
Return a dictionary of available Ansys unified installation versions with their base paths.

# Returns

dict[int: str]

: Return all Ansys unified installations paths in Windows.

# Notes

On Windows, It uses the environment variable `AWP_ROOTXXX`.

The student versions are returned at the end of the dict and
with negative value for the version.

# Examples

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import get_available_ansys_installations
>>> get_available_ansys_installations()
{222: 'C:\Program Files\ANSYS Inc\v222',
 212: 'C:\Program Files\ANSYS Inc\v212',
 -222: 'C:\Program Files\ANSYS Inc\ANSYS Student\v222'}
```

Return all installed Ansys paths in Linux.

```pycon
>>> get_available_ansys_installations()
{194: '/usr/ansys_inc/v194',
 202: '/usr/ansys_inc/v202',
 211: '/usr/ansys_inc/v211'}
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._get_unified_install_base_for_version
documentation:
Search for the unified install of a given version from the supported versions.

Returns
-------
Tuple[str, str]
    The base unified install path and version

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.find_mechanical
documentation:
Search for the Mechanical path in the standard installation location.

# Returns

mechanical_path

: Full path to the executable file for the latest Mechanical version.

version

: Version in the float format. For example, `23.1` for 2023 R1.
  If no version has be found, version is set to ""

# Examples

On Windows:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import find_mechanical
>>> find_mechanical()
('C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v231/aisol/bin/winx64/AnsysWBU.exe', 23.1)
```

On Linux:

```pycon
>>> find_mechanical()
('/usr/ansys_inc/v231/aisol/.workbench', 23.1)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.find_mapdl
documentation:
Searches for Ansys MAPDL path within the standard install location
and returns the path of the latest version.

# Parameters

version

: Version of Ansys MAPDL to search for.
  If using `int`, it should follow the convention `XXY`, where
  `XX` is the major version,
  and `Y` is the minor.
  If using `float`, it should follow the convention `XX.Y`, where
  `XX` is the major version,
  and `Y` is the minor.
  If `None`, use latest available version on the machine.

# Returns

ansys_path

: Full path to ANSYS executable.

version

: Version float. For example, 21.1 corresponds to 2021R1.

# Examples

Within Windows

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import find_mapdl
>>> find_mapdl()
'C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v211/ANSYS/bin/winx64/ansys211.exe', 21.1
```

Within Linux

```pycon
>>> find_mapdl()
(/usr/ansys_inc/v211/ansys/bin/ansys211, 21.1)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.find_dyna
documentation:
Searches for Ansys LS-Dyna path within the standard install location
and returns the path of the latest version.

# Parameters

version

: Version of Ansys LS-Dyna to search for.
  If using `int`, it should follow the convention `XXY`, where
  `XX` is the major version,
  and `Y` is the minor.
  If using `float`, it should follow the convention `XX.Y`, where
  `XX` is the major version,
  and `Y` is the minor.
  If `None`, use latest available version on the machine.

# Returns

ansys_path

: Full path to Ansys LS-Dyna executable.

version

: Version float. For example, 21.1 corresponds to 2021R1.

# Examples

Within Windows

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import find_dyna
>>> find_dyna()
'C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v232/ANSYS/bin/winx64/LSDYNA232.exe', 23.2
```

Within Linux

```pycon
>>> find_dyna()
(/usr/ansys_inc/v232/ansys/bin/lsdyna232, 23.2)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.find_ansys
documentation: Obsolete method, use find_mapdl.

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.change_default_mapdl_path
documentation:
Change your default Ansys MAPDL path.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: Ansys MAPDL executable path. Must be a full path.

# Examples

Change default Ansys MAPDL location on Linux

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import change_default_mapdl_path, get_mapdl_path
>>> change_default_mapdl_path('/ansys_inc/v201/ansys/bin/ansys201')
>>> get_mapdl_path()
'/ansys_inc/v201/ansys/bin/ansys201'
```

Change default Ansys location on Windows

```pycon
>>> mapdl_path = 'C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v193/ansys/bin/winx64/ANSYS193.exe'
>>> change_default_mapdl_path(mapdl_path)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.change_default_dyna_path
documentation:
Change your default Ansys LS-Dyna path.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: path to LS-Dyna executable. Must be a full path. This need not contain the name of the executable,
  because the name of the LS-Dyna executable depends on the precision.

# Examples

Change default Ansys LS-Dyna location on Linux

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import change_default_dyna_path, get_dyna_path
>>> change_default_dyna_path('/ansys_inc/v232/ansys/bin/lsdyna232')
>>> get_dyna_path()
'/ansys_inc/v232/ansys/bin/lsdyna232'
```

Change default Ansys LS-Dyna location on Windows

```pycon
>>> dyna_path = 'C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v232/ansys/bin/winx64/LSDYNA232.exe'
>>> change_default_dyna_path(dyna_path)
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.change_default_mechanical_path
documentation:
Change your default Mechanical path.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: Full path for the Mechanical executable file to use.

# Examples

On Windows:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import change_default_mechanical_path, get_mechanical_path
>>> change_default_mechanical_path('C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v231/aisol/bin/win64/AnsysWBU.exe')
>>> get_mechanical_path()
'C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v231/aisol/bin/win64/AnsysWBU.exe'
```

On Linux:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import change_default_mechanical_path, get_mechanical_path
>>> change_default_mechanical_path('/ansys_inc/v231/aisol/.workbench')
>>> get_mechanical_path()
'/ansys_inc/v231/aisol/.workbench'
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.change_default_ansys_path
documentation: Deprecated, use `change_default_mapdl_path` instead

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.save_mechanical_path
documentation:
Find the Mechanical path or query user.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: Path for the Mechanical executable file (`AnsysWBU.exe`).
  The default is `None`, in which case an attempt is made to
  obtain the path from the following sources in this order:

  - The default Mechanical paths (for example,
    `C:/Program Files/Ansys Inc/vXXX/aiso/bin/AnsysWBU.exe`)
  - The configuration file
  - User input

  If a path is supplied, this method performs some checks. If the
  checks are successful, it writes this path to the configuration
  file.

# Returns

str

: Path for the Mechanical executable file.

# Notes

The location of the configuration file `config.txt` can be found in
`ansys.tools.path.SETTINGS_DIR`. For example:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import SETTINGS_DIR
>>> import os
>>> print(os.path.join(SETTINGS_DIR, "config.txt"))
C:/Users/[username]]/AppData/Local/Ansys/ansys_tools_path/config.txt
```

You can change the default for the `exe_loc` parameter either by modifying the
`config.txt` file or by running this code:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import save_mechanical_path
>>> save_mechanical_path("/new/path/to/executable")
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.save_dyna_path
documentation:
Find Ansys LD-Dyna's path or query user.

If no `exe_loc` argument is supplied, this function attempt
to obtain the Ansys LS-Dyna executable from (and in order):

- The default ansys paths (i.e. `'C:/Program Files/Ansys Inc/vXXX/ansys/bin/winx64/LSDYNAXXX'`)
- The configuration file
- User input

If `exe_loc` is supplied, this function does some checks.
If successful, it will write that `exe_loc` into the config file.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: Path of the LS-Dyna executable ('lsdynaXXX'), by default `None`.

# Returns

str

: Path of the LS-Dyna executable.

# Notes

The location of the configuration file `config.txt` can be found in
`ansys.tools.path.SETTINGS_DIR`. For example:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import SETTINGS_DIR
>>> import os
>>> print(os.path.join(SETTINGS_DIR, "config.txt"))
C:/Users/[username]/AppData/Local/Ansys/ansys_tools_path/config.txt
```

# Examples

You can change the default `exe_loc` either by modifying the mentioned
`config.txt` file or by executing:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import save_dyna_path
>>> save_dyna_path('/new/path/to/executable')
```

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.save_mapdl_path
documentation:
Find Ansys MAPDL's path or query user.

If no `exe_loc` argument is supplied, this function attempt
to obtain the Ansys MAPDL executable from (and in order):

- The default ansys paths (i.e. `'C:/Program Files/Ansys Inc/vXXX/ansys/bin/winx64/ansysXXX'`)
- The configuration file
- User input

If `exe_loc` is supplied, this function does some checks.
If successful, it will write that `exe_loc` into the config file.

# Parameters

exe_loc

: Path of the MAPDL executable ('ansysXXX'), by default `None`.

# Returns

str

: Path of the MAPDL executable.

# Notes

The location of the configuration file `config.txt` can be found in
`ansys.tools.path.SETTINGS_DIR`. For example:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import SETTINGS_DIR
>>> import os
>>> print(os.path.join(SETTINGS_DIR, "config.txt"))
C:/Users/[username]/AppData/Local/Ansys/ansys_tools_path/config.txt
```

# Examples

You can change the default `exe_loc` either by modifying the mentioned
`config.txt` file or by executing:

```pycon
>>> from ansys.tools.path import save_mapdl_path
>>> save_mapdl_path('/new/path/to/executable')
```

documentation: Clear the entry of the specified product in the configuration file

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_mapdl_path
documentation:
Acquires Ansys MAPDL Path:

First, it looks in the configuration file, used by `save_mapdl_path`
Then, it tries to find it based on conventions for where it usually is.
Lastly, it takes user input

# Parameters

allow_input

: Allow user input to find Ansys MAPDL path. The default is `True`.

version

: Version of Ansys MAPDL to search for. For example `version=22.2`.
  If `None`, use latest.

find: bool, optional

: Allow ansys-tools-path to search for Ansys Mechanical in typical installation locations

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_dyna_path
documentation:
Acquires Ansys LS-Dyna Path from a cached file or user input

First, it looks in the configuration file, used by `save_dyna_path`
Then, it tries to find it based on conventions for where it usually is.
Lastly, it takes user input

# Parameters

allow_input

: Allow user input to find Ansys LS-Dyna path. The default is `True`.

version

: Version of Ansys LS-Dyna to search for. For example `version=22.2`.
  If `None`, use latest.

find: bool, optional

: Allow ansys-tools-path to search for Ansys Mechanical in typical installation locations

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_ansys_path
documentation: Deprecated, use `get_mapdl_path` instead

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_mechanical_path
documentation:
Acquires Ansys Mechanical Path

First, it looks in the configuration file, used by `save_mechanical_path`
Then, it tries to find it based on conventions for where it usually is.
Lastly, it takes user input

# Parameters

allow_input

: Allow user input to find Ansys Mechanical path. The default is `True`.

version

: Version of Ansys Mechanical to search for. For example `version=22.2`.
  If `None`, use latest.

find: bool, optional

: Allow ansys-tools-path to search for Ansys Mechanical in typical installation locations

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._mechanical_version_from_path
documentation:
Extract the Ansys Mechanical version from a path.

Generally, the version of Mechanical is contained in the path:

- On Windows, for example: `C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v231/aisol/bin/winx64/AnsysWBU.exe`
- On Linux, for example: `/usr/ansys_inc/v231/aisol/.workbench`

# Parameters

path

: Path to the Mechanical executable file.

# Returns

int

: Integer version number (for example, 231).

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path._mapdl_version_from_path
documentation:
Extract ansys version from a path.  Generally, the version of
Ansys MAPDL is contained in the path:
C:/Program Files/ANSYS Inc/v202/ansys/bin/winx64/ANSYS202.exe
/usr/ansys_inc/v211/ansys/bin/mapdl
Note that if the Ansys MAPDL executable, you have to rely on the version
in the path.
Parameters
----------
path : str
    Path to the Ansys MAPDL executable

Returns
-------
int
    Integer version number (e.g. 211).

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.version_from_path
documentation:
Extract the product version from a path.

Parameters
----------
path : str
    Path to the executable file.

Returns
-------
int
    Integer version number (for example, 231).

functions.name: ansys.tools.path.path.get_latest_ansys_installation
documentation:
Return a tuple with the latest ansys installation version and its path

If there is a student version and a regular installation for the latest release, the regular one is returned

Returns
-------
Tuple[int, str]
    Tuple with the latest version and path of the installation

Raise
-----
ValueError
    No Ansys installation found
